did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a transfer learning-based deep learning ensemble framework combining Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet121, InceptionV3, Xception) and Vision Transformers (ViT) to perform melanoma image classification and content-based image retrieval (CBIR) for dermatological diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a transfer learning-based deep learning ensemble framework for melanoma diagnosis and image retrieval. It details specific methods (using DenseNet121, InceptionV3, ViT, Xception), datasets (Kaggle melanoma dataset), and results (95.25% accuracy, 0.9538 mAP), which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Transfer Learning-Based Ensemble of CNNs and Vision Transformers for Accurate Melanoma Diagnosis and Image Retrieval
brief_summary: This study proposes a transfer learning-based deep learning ensemble framework to automate melanoma image classification and enhance content-based image retrieval (CBIR) systems. The authors utilized pre-trained models, including DenseNet121, InceptionV3, Xception, and Vision Transformer (ViT), to extract deep features which were integrated via a weighted fusion strategy. The system was evaluated on the Kaggle melanoma dataset using classification accuracy and mean Average Precision (mAP) metrics. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed Ensemble model significantly outperformed standalone models, achieving a classification accuracy of 95.25% and an mAP score of 0.9538. These findings indicate that integrating diverse model architectures improves diagnostic accuracy and retrieval effectiveness for early melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the classification and retrieval tasks. The study focuses on automating the diagnosis of melanoma, an aggressive skin cancer, using the Kaggle melanoma dataset.

Referenced in the context of the dataset source (Kaggle melanoma dataset, which typically contains benign/malignant classes) and in citations (e.g., Ref 11, 18) discussing classification of melanoma vs. non-melanoma or benign lesions. The paper's specific experiments focus on melanoma classification/retrieval, implying a binary or melanoma-centric task within the dataset context.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of melanoma images using an ensemble of CNNs and Vision Transformers. The target is discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant). Performance was evaluated using accuracy (95.25%), precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.

The paper also performs Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR) to retrieve clinically meaningful similar cases for each query image. Performance was evaluated using mean Average Precision (mAP) of 0.9538, PR curves, and cosine similarity scores.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset obtained from Kaggle, consisting of dermoscopic images of melanoma lesions. The dataset is described as a single-center, retrospective dataset with standardized imaging conditions.

use.training: Used for training the transfer learning models (DenseNet121, InceptionV3, ViT, Xception) with real-time data augmentation.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the generalization performance of the models for classification and content-based image retrieval (CBIR).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate 95% confidence intervals for accuracy and mAP metrics to demonstrate the reliability and variance of performance metrics on the test data.
To provide a range of values for the mean accuracy and mAP scores, calculated using bootstrap sampling (n=100 iterations) and the z-score method (1.96).
To evaluate the discrimination capability of individual models (DenseNet121, InceptionV3, ViT, Xception) via AUC values.
To calculate precision, recall, F1-score, and analyze misclassification rates (false positives/negatives) for detailed performance evaluation.
To analyze the stability and accuracy of the system's performance across different recall levels for the CBIR task.
To measure the similarity between feature vectors for ranking retrieved images in the Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR) system.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper explicitly states that traditional statistical significance testing (e.g., McNemar’s test or paired t-tests) was not performed because the experimental setup used a single, fixed train/test split, which does not yield meaningful variance estimates for such tests. Instead, the authors used bootstrap sampling to calculate 95% confidence intervals to demonstrate the reliability of performance metrics.

classification_results.notes: The Ensemble model significantly outperformed standalone models. DenseNet121 and Xception showed superior clinical reliability with high sensitivity and specificity. ViT exhibited lower performance and higher false negative rates.

cbir_results.notes: Results based on 20 random queries. DenseNet121 and Xception retrieved visually similar lesions correctly. InceptionV3 and ViT produced erroneous matches in some cases, confusing heterogeneous pigmented lesions with benign samples. The Multi-model fusion approach achieved the highest mAP.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of DenseNet121, InceptionV3, Vision Transformer (ViT), and Xception
architecture_used.pretraining: All base models (DenseNet121, InceptionV3, ViT, Xception) were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: DenseNet121 and ViT: 224x224 pixels; InceptionV3 and Xception: 299x299 pixels

added_layers: 3x Conv2D layers (256, 256, 128 filters) with ReLU activation

training_phases: Phase 1: All layers frozen, 4x Conv2D (512, 512, 256, 128 filters), MaxPooling2D, BatchNormalization, GlobalAveragePooling2D, Dense (512, ReLU), Dropout (50%), Sigmoid output
Phase 2 (Fine-tuning): First 150 layers frozen, remaining layers (layers 151–mixed10) unfrozen
added_layers: 4x Conv2D layers (512, 512, 256, 128 filters) with ReLU activation
3x Conv2D layers (256, 256, 128 filters) with ReLU activation

architecture_details.ensemble_method: Weighted fusion strategy (feature-level fusion) combining deep feature representations from all four models

optimizer.InceptionV3: Adamax (Phase 1), Adamax (Phase 2 fine-tuning with lower LR)

callbacks: EarlyStopping (patience=10 for DenseNet121/Xception/ViT; unspecified for InceptionV3 but mentioned)

classification_or_output_head.loss_function: Binary Cross-entropy (implied by sigmoid output and binary classification task)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Images were rescaled to 299x299 pixels for Xception and InceptionV3 models, and to 224x224 pixels for DenseNet121 and ViT models.

Real-time data augmentation using Keras ImageDataGenerator during training, including: random rotations (±15°), width/height shifts (10%), shear (0.2), zoom range (0.1), and brightness adjustments (range 0.8–1.2).

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random rotations (±15°), width/height shifts (10%), and shear (0.2) applied using Keras ImageDataGenerator.
Zoom range (0.1) and brightness adjustments (range 0.8–1.2) applied using Keras ImageDataGenerator.
Applied during training using Keras ImageDataGenerator to improve generalization.

conclusions: The proposed transfer learning-based ensemble framework, which integrates deep features from DenseNet121, InceptionV3, Vision Transformer (ViT), and Xception via weighted fusion, significantly outperforms standalone models in both melanoma classification and content-based image retrieval (CBIR).
The ensemble model achieved a classification accuracy of 95.25% and a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 0.9538 for CBIR, demonstrating high diagnostic accuracy and retrieval effectiveness.

Among individual models, DenseNet121 and Xception provided superior and clinically reliable results with high sensitivity and specificity, whereas the ViT model exhibited lower performance and a higher false negative rate, suggesting potential inadequacy in detecting malignant cases.

In CBIR tasks, DenseNet121 and Xception successfully retrieved visually similar lesions, while InceptionV3 and ViT produced erroneous matches by confusing heterogeneous pigmented lesions with benign samples or overfocusing on artifacts like specular highlights.
The integration of diverse model architectures improves feature generalization and robustness, offering a multifunctional platform for similarity-based case comparison and clinical decision support.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the use of a single, fixed train/test split from the Kaggle dataset, which precluded traditional statistical significance testing (e.g., McNemar’s test) and k-fold cross-validation; however, reliability was demonstrated using 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap sampling.

Future improvements for CBIR robustness should include preprocessing to remove hair and glare artifacts (e.g., using inpainting or DullRazor) and employing boundary-aware descriptors to better handle irregular edges and texture.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the Kaggle Melanoma Cancer Image dataset, which is a single-center, retrospective dataset with standardized imaging conditions, limiting the capture of real-world diversity in skin tone, lighting, and device-specific artifacts.
External validation on heterogeneous datasets (e.g., ISIC archive) and multiple clinical centers is identified as a necessary future step to ensure generalizability and clinical relevance.

The experimental setup used a single, fixed train/test split, which precluded traditional statistical significance testing (e.g., McNemar’s test) and k-fold cross-validation; reliability was instead demonstrated using 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap sampling.

The proposed Ensemble model is explicitly designed as a diagnostic support system rather than a ready-for-deployment solution; system-level performance aspects such as inference time and memory consumption were not evaluated.

The ensemble fusion introduces architectural complexity that may not be optimal for real-time applications; future work plans to address this via model compression (pruning, quantization) and deployment optimizations (TensorRT, ONNX).

The Vision Transformer (ViT) model exhibited lower performance (88.25% accuracy) and higher false negative rates compared to CNNs, suggesting potential inadequacy in detecting malignant cases within this specific ensemble context.

In CBIR tasks, InceptionV3 and ViT produced erroneous matches by confusing heterogeneous pigmented lesions with benign samples or overfocusing on artifacts like specular highlights, whereas DenseNet121 and Xception performed more robustly.

Future improvements for CBIR robustness should include preprocessing to remove hair and glare artifacts (e.g., using inpainting or DullRazor) and employing boundary-aware descriptors to better handle irregular edges and texture.
